speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor Jack Pomerantz. This is tape number five.

speaker: interviewer
text: By 1946, when you were in Salzburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You already knew that you wanted to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in the next three years between 1946 and '49?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Yes. I was sleeping with my girlfriend in the house with her parents-- not together. But I had a room there. We was-- my sister got out of the hospital and my brother-in-law. And they went to Bergen-Belsen from Salzburg. And I stayed already in Salzburg with a girl, which I planned to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they just-- I don't know for what reason they wanted to go. They had a lot of friends there who survived to-- they got know they are there. And naturally, I had my own life already after. And at that time, I decided I want to go visiting [? then. ?] So I went to the Austrian-- to the German border. I crossed over the border. Because you're not allowed to travel as a, as a refugee from one country to another.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went there. And I'm on the train going to Bergen-Belsen. On the train, I meet a fella. The fella-- I started to talk to him. And he says to me, you wouldn't believe it, Jack. I live next to your sister. And he said, is your brother-in-law Haskiel and everything? And everything was right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, you in troubles maybe. Let me give you a document. I got a document. And I'll see you tonight. He got off. He said, I'm getting off here for business. And you go further. As he walks-- gets off, a man walks over to me in a couple miles later. He says to me, I'm gonna put you in jail. See, I was talking to him in Polish. There was a Polish man-- I don't know if he was Polish or Ukrainian. And he listened to everything, that I am illegal there. And he said, you're going to get me your money. Or I'm going to put you in jail. You've got a false document. I said to him, I don't have a lot of money. Whatever I got, I give it to you. I had, maybe, $20, $30. And I said, I'll give it to you. No, he wants more. And I could see I got problems. So I said to him, I got money sewed into my cuffs in my pants. If you get off the train, I'll, I'll take it out and give it to you. We got off the train in Hanover, I think-- oh, no, Kassel. That's right, Kassel. We got off in Kassel. And behind the train station is a building, four walls bombed out on the [INAUDIBLE] there. I go inside. But I haven't got any money. While he's bending down to go to my cuffs, I pick up a brick. Young I was. And I was very strong. And I hit him with the brick. And I hit him with the brick. He started to bleed. And he fell down. It's long, he gave me a chance to get out of there. As I got out. I ran a couple corners right and left, right and left, that nobody would know what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I'm walking there, I asked somebody, where do you sleep over if you have no money? He said to me, there's a monastery. You go two blocks. Go to the right. And there's a monastery. And you can eat, sleep there. And you know, they'll give you-- and I went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went there. And I slept over. And they were talking to me. And they said to me, there's a camp in the town. And they gonna get me blankets and money. I should go talk to some Jews, if they want to convert to, to Christianity. I promised them the moon. I'm a good salesman. And I took some money. And I took some stuff-- blankets. And I'm walking on the street again. I finally see a guy. And I look at him. And I [? thought ?] maybe it's the right guy. And I tell him, I got a little problem. He [INAUDIBLE], he tells me, [GERMAN]. I knew right away, I'm in the right track. I tell him the whole story from A to Z. He said, I'll tell you what I'll do for you. I don't want you to go on the train, nothing. I'll give you a taxi. And the taxi is gonna bring you to, to, I think, [INAUDIBLE]. He knew already where it is. And he took me back to the Austrian border. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And I crossed in-- in-- By [INAUDIBLE] is already the DP camp. I'm home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I went over. And I went to Salzburg home. OK, I assume the guy-- I find out later-- went home at night time and said, where's your brother-- to my sister. Said, what  brother? Said, I gave your brother a document. They start to accuse me. He accuses my sister that I'm not her brother, I'm a crook. I took the passport from her. Because where am I? So my brother-in-law said he has to find out what's going on. You know, I didn't have telephone or anything I can talk to them. He got on a train. He came to Salzburg. He find out the true story of what happened. Then, I went with him back to Bergen-Belsen. I was there two days. And then I went back. Because I was afraid. Because if they catch you and you on the list doing the wrong things, then you couldn't go to the United States. You follow me? Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so that's it. And I was living in Salzburg to the day when it has to go-- when we have to leave for the United States. I leave to the United States. I come to the United States and took me the first night. I'm crying the whole night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spent the years in Salzburg until you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm in Salzburg till I came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you left--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to make fast. Because we're [? having ?] [? good ?] time. When I got on the ship--

speaker: interviewer
text: But I want to ask, you left behind--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sylvia.

speaker: interviewer
text: The, the woman who you had married.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not married then.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she came a message to me that she is arrested on the border. Because she wants to go with me. I had a couple guys with me who gave me a needle to put me to sleep. I wanted to get off. And I say, I'm not going along. I'm going back. I love her. You know, I've been in love. And they gave me a needle. And they was talking to me. And somehow, they got me in on the boat. And I left. And I'm in the United States. And soon, I come to the United States. My aunt in Brooklyn is, to me, a stranger. My cousins are strangers. And I feel my life is disgusted. They right away noticed in me, there's something wrong with me. I picked myself up after six months. I went to Washington. And I got a visa to go back to Europe. But they talked me into get a reentry permit, which I got it. I went back to Europe. And I got married. And I'm waiting for her to get a visa to come with me. And I'm-- and I, I get to a point-- I keep selling everything I got, a ring, a suit, anything to make a living. But then I said, OK, I'm leaving. And I'm gonna go back to the United States. And I'm gonna go to work. And when I-- whatever I make, I'll send her some money to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm going. And I line up in Paris. I line up in Paris. And I'm waiting to go on a boat. I don't know if you know anything-- Champs Elysees in Paris. Is a hotel there. And it's called a United Jewish Appeal, that's all. They get me money for cigarettes and food and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyways, there comes a girl-- a fella. And I become friendly with this guy. And this fella says to me, Jack, there's tomorrow coming a girl. You're gonna die seeing her. And she's my friend. And he, and he said he's taking her out tonight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I don't care if you come home 2 o'clock in the morning, 3 o'clock in the morning, knock on the door, wake me up, and tell me how did everything went. He come back. And he said, you know something? I couldn't kiss her. That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm gonna make this story short. It was a short time in the hotel. She moved in with me. And I was living with her there. When came time to go, she went to Canada. She went to Canada. Me and a Hungarian couple took her to the train to go to the boat to go to Canada. When she left to Canada, and I'm going home. And me and the Hungarian couple are going to United States. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we are-- this guy looks at me-- the Hungarian-- and he says, Jack, I'm gonna do you a favor. As we on the, on the water on the boat, he pays for me-- he knows I haven't got any money-- to, to call up the other ship. And I talked to her on the telephone, from one ship to another. She went there. But she knew I'm married. She went to Canada. And I went to, to the United States. And then she kept writing me letters. And I told her, you know, I am married. I am married and I'm not gonna-- that's the end of it. And I said, don't-- just find somebody, get married, and be happy. And that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, when I got-- when I find out that Sylvia, my wife, cannot come here, we got-- we-- I applied for divorce. Meanwhile, I'm meeting my, my wife. They keep talking about wedding. And I cannot tell them I'm not divorced. So each time, her mother keep asking me. And I kept having-- giving an excuse.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no money for furniture. I have no money for, for an apartment. But each time, she keeps-- and I can't-- I don't have a way out anymore And I finally agreed the wedding would be August the 1st, 1953. And, and I-- my luck is running, all my life, wonderful. The day before the wedding-- this was on a Saturday-- Friday, I call up my landlady in Brooklyn-- South 52nd Street, Brooklyn. She said, Jack, you-- she called me a name-- you a son-of-a-- because, she said, you're so lucky. You got the divorce in the mail. [LAUGHS] 24 hours before the wedding, I got the divorce in the mail. OK. Everything-- we got married. We got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing at this time now?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to the United States, I was working as a furniture finisher. But took me a short time to work myself up. I started to work for $25 a week. A guy was working with me who made $60 a week. And I said to myself, if it comes the day that I'll make $60 a week, I be writing to Europe that I own the United States. Well, it took me six months. And I made $100 a week. And the guy still were making $60 a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everything went for me beautiful. And when we got married, I went to-- for my honeymoon to Canada. We're sitting in Canada in a restaurant. And a couple sits next to us. And she said, you know, I love you accent. From where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell her. And she-- and I tell her a story. I'm looking for a girl in Canada. It's like looking a needle in the ocean. And I tell her the whole story. And you gonna hear after talking to my wife, will repeat that. And she said, you wouldn't believe it, Jack. I have a neighbor who told me a story about a boy she had-- a boyfriend she had in Paris. And everything foreseen, that's unbelievable. I'm gonna take you there. Me and my-- she took us around all Sunday, this couple. And they showed us Toronto-- very lovely. And they took us around wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: We go home. We have lunch in her house. She calls up the neighbor. Me and my wife and are walking over to the neighbor and her husband, and guess what? This is the woman already who has two children with her husband. And I cannot describe you what happened. [LAUGHS] And my wife took it beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, I'm going to Europe. And I take to my wife to Europe. And we going to-- we are in Lienz. I pick up the phone in Lienz. And I call my wife who I divorced. My wife is in bed, is getting an instant migraine headache. This girl is coming with her mother and picking me and my wife up and takin' us to the villa-- gorgeous villa, beautiful-- takin' us to the villa. And they treating us-- I cannot describe you. And she becomes with my wife like two sisters. The only thing she was complaining with my wife that I am not such a sweet boy. And, and, and I went that time, I remember, I left her $2,000 without my wife knowing. But my wife said to me, I don't care. I know you left the money. And I don't care how much you left her. But leave her a little more. And I gave her another $1,000. And I told that to my wife. She said, you did the nicest thing in your life. When you meet my wife for five minutes, you gonna know who I'm talking about. If there's a Jewish saint, she should be a saint. She's a--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have a wonderful family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful, a wonderful human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your daughters.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughters-- so I had two daughters. One daughter-- and I remember when I had the two daughters, two girls. I was dreaming. It's, to me, is my pleasure. And one grew up-- I send them both to college-- and she went to college in Burlington, the University of Burlington. And she met a boyfriend there. And she got married there. I started out working as a furniture finisher. From a furniture finisher, I went and-- I went into a furniture store. I opened a furniture store. And it didn't work out so right. So I quit the furniture store. And I went into building. And I took a second mortgage on my house-- $10,000-- and I went into building. And I never been a big builder. But I been successful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very small builder, because I build very, very beautiful homes, like $2 million, you know? And my mind, it was for creation. I like to create things. And I, I would put up one house a year even. It didn't bother me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was something-- it had to be something-- I'll show you later pictures I got. You'll see what I mean. Like, in one of the houses, was an atrium. Trees were growing in the middle of the house with waterfalls in the middle of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've been successful in your business.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and then-- I was successful in my business.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've been successful, obviously, in this marriage, with your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very successful.

speaker: interviewer
text: You now have--

speaker: interviewee
text: With my children-- I'm not exactly sure. [LAUGHTER] But with my wife, is I cannot describe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, you have three grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got three grandchildren, which I love 'em. I wish I would love my children as I love my grandchildren. And, and otherwise, everything's wonderful. I have-- a niece is comin' Thursday from, from London to have Passover with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you-- your grandchildren are very young now. When they get older, when they hear the whole story of what happened to you, what happened to their family, to their grandparents, great-grandparents, to the uncles and aunts, what, what, what message do you have for them about the experience that you went through, having lost your parents, three of your siblings, having been so close to death so many times during those years? What do you want them to know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandson is seven years old. He'll tell you everything about me already. My granddaughter is four years old-- five years old and going on 15. They are so educated that they can go in, in a, in a grade-- like the girl five years can go in a seven, eight-year grade. They are writing and reading. Because my daughter and the son-in-law, first of all, she bringin' 'em up Jewish. And the, the father is bringin' 'em every Sunday to Hebrew school. The father is, is one of the best husbands in the world you can find, the best father in the world. When I met my son-in-law, he was a laborer for $4 an hour. I send him to college. And he's a lawyer now. And he is a lawyer in a firm in Vermont. And he's a partner already. And at nighttime, he gives-- he gives-- he teaches in college, like a professor. And he gets $75 an hour for, for-- because he's got a brain as a computer-- unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these are all bright people. Your grandchildren are very intelligent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've told them these stories. But what-- is there a message in all of this about--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- yeah, there is a message.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is, they are very ethical. They are very-- I can write a book just about my daughter, my son-in-law, and my children. Like, for an example, in the University of Vermont, this-- in the next week is gonna be a program. It's called Holocaust Survivors. And they're also going there. The message for them is, is it doesn't-- first of all, I feel the same way if you Jewish or non-Jewish. My always feeling is to judge the, the person who he is, not what religion he is, and not what-- it-- it to-- to be nice. To give an example, all my friends are Christians. 99% my friends are Christians. And I don't know how to explain you. And, and the same thing, I didn't ask my son-in-law, the children to have Jewish. He himself suggested to my wife. I didn't ask him to, to, to convert to Judaism. As long they are happy, that's what I was interested in. In [INAUDIBLE], I cannot describe you. There's no words. And he's very intelligent. And he's very bright. I wouldn't say street bright, but book-- very bright. He can read a book in a month. He will tell you every word what's in the book. You can discuss with him about anything in  the world. He's that smart. He is.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you told us that you didn't have regular education. You didn't go to real school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have any education.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had a life experience that very few people have.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had experience. I, I, I-- my wife, my people said, I should be a surgeon. I should be a lawyer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess I'm born with a certain things in me. I just, I don't know how to explain that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you think all of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can design my own plans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think those characteristics--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helped you to survive what you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, oh, yes. But I also tried in my life-- when I know when we came to Siberia, in no time, they made me for the head for the distribution of the food. Because never try to be smarter than you next door neighbor or your friend. Or never try to, to, to be on the top of him, that you know more or whatever it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was given a job to do, I did my job. I remember when they called out, how many people are sick? A lot of people, Jewish people, hold up their hands, they are sick. I didn't. They gave me something to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I suffered horrible. I did it. When I was given to distribution, I would never take anything from one person to give to another person, for nothing in the world. I did my job. And the same thing when I got to know a man in New Jersey by the name of Jim Wallwork. He was senator four times. And he entrusted totally his life in me. And he is-- I cannot describe you. His daughter is an author. She writes also for A son-- his son-in-law is just finishing a book, which is being published right now. The book is being published. And it's about politics in the '80s. And it's mostly about [? Kirkpatrick, ?] and, and Reagan, and, and the final secretary-- I forgot-- Baker, about a lot of people. In fact, they are coming here also for Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've met interesting people right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my Christian families are gonna-- pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: You've met very interesting people in your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have, I have here coming three Christian families are having Seder with me every year. And some of them are angry because I haven't [INAUDIBLE] a place, a dining room enough to get everybody here. And I am very happy. I am, I'm, I'm not a rich person, in a sense-- when you look at it, you would take me much richer. Because life to me is more important than money. But as long I have enough to live my life for what I need, to, to not-- to buy food, all other things doesn't interest me, like to have, to have yachts or anything. It doesn't mean nothing to me. I'd rather have a lot of friends then have a-- that's the way I am. And I'm, and I'm lucky. I'm lucky. Even when I meet a doctor-- I have a heart specialist. He gave me his telephone. You cannot make an appointment three months ahead. He said, any minute, call me in my house. He became my best friend. And everyone I sold a house to, if you could see it, how they are to me, you would say-- you wouldn't believe me, if you would see the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you consider yourself a lucky person and a happy person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a happy person.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I also say, it's up to you to do it. As I said before, I came, I worked seven days a week. I worked on three jobs. I came home every night, 11 o'clock at night. I got up 6:00 in the morning. When my wife was sleeping on Sunday, I went to work to homes to polish furniture to make $40 for the day, $30. And I didn't complain. I was happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the results are obvious.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when you see my wife, you'll see how happy she is.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're gonna meet her now. And I thank you for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a pleasure to have you here. And I was very happy to get it-- I could say, to get it off my chest. Because I like to talk about it. Like people like to say how rich they used to be, I know I say how poor I used to be. And I'm proud to what I made out of myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're now joined by Mrs. Pomerantz. And we have heard your husband's whole story. And he said wonderful things about you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you have anything you want to say about his life story, his accomplishments, what he's been through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he's certainly come, come a long way from-- after the war, he came here with nothing. And he accomplished quite a bit. We have two daughters and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And a lovely wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: We live-- and we-- he made, made a nice living. And we live well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he describes himself as a happy man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and we are both very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we thank you for contributing to this story and to this project.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thanks.
